title: GEMINI-3 HIL REFERENCE TRANSLATIONS — THE DIAMOND ARM (1968)

original.rus: Ребята, на его месте должен был быть… я! — Напьёшься — будешь

reference.eng: Guys, I should have been in his place! — Get drunk enough, and you will be!

items.analysis: A clash of registers. Gorbunkov is in full 'spy thriller' mode; he sees a body being carried and assumes the smugglers have struck again. His statement is a heavy, sacrificial declaration of a hero who thinks he dodged a bullet. The policeman, performing a routine 'drunk tank' pickup, is bored and amused. His response is a classic piece of 'cop humor'—mocking the dramatic tone by treating 'being carried away' as an inevitable destination reached through enough alcohol consumption.

original.rus: Я не трус… но я боюсь

reference.eng: I'm no coward... but I'm overpowered by fear!

items.analysis: A relatable human paradox that separates moral character ('not a coward') from the physiological reality of fear ('but I am afraid'). It captures the 'little man' forced into a 'big situation' who maintains his dignity while admitting his vulnerability. The rhythmic 'Trus/Boyus' rhyme in Russian provides a 'bouncing' effect that makes the admission feel lighthearted and endearing rather than pathetic. ***Things to avoid:*** literal translation that does not create any comic effect.

original.rus: У Вас… ус… отклеился

reference.eng: Um... your... mustache... is peeling off.

items.analysis: The ultimate exposure of the villain's facade through Gorbunkov's polite, almost helpful honesty in a moment of high tension. Pointing out a trivial failure, like a loose fake mustache, completely undermines the criminal's 'professionalism' and highlights the absurdity of their attempts at intrigue.

original.rus: Дитя́м — мороженое, его бабе — цветы. Смотры, нэ пэрэпутай!.. Кутузоу!

reference.eng: Ice cream for the young'uns, flowers for his broad. Look now, don't mix 'em up! Kutuzov!

items.analysis: Lyolik uses his characteristic speech style (non-standard, uneducated-sounding) to micro-manage Gena, who he (correctly) thinks so absent-minded that he might give the flowers to the children and ice cream to the wife. Calling the bandaged Gena 'Kutuzov' adds a layer of sarcastic historical mockery, comparing the bungling smuggler to the one-eyed Napoleonic-era General.

original.rus: Как говорит наш дорогой шеф, за чужой счёт пьют даже трезвенники… и язвенники!

reference.eng: As our dear boss says, on someone else's dime, even teetotalers and convalescers drink!

items.analysis: Cynical underworld philosophy suggesting greed is the ultimate human motivator, overriding both moral and medical convictions. The original Russian uses identical suffixes and rhythmic weight (trezvenniki/yazvenniki) to create a balanced, proverbial 'law of nature' feel regarding the allure of free alcohol. ***Things to avoid:*** no rythm whatsover; at least some creative solution must be present.

examples.1:
As our dear boss says,
even teetotalers drink on someone else’s dime
and ulcer patients too.
examples.2:

original.rus: Не виноватая я! Он сам пришёл!

reference.eng: Well, if you're ever in Kolyma... — No, better you visit us!

items.analysis: A joke based on Soviet geography. Kolyma was synonymous with labor camps; while the guest offers a sincere invitation from his home region, Gena hears it as a threat of imprisonment. His polite, reflexive rejection is a classic cultural response to perceived danger.

reference.eng: — You wouldn't have the same one, but with mother-of-pearl buttons? — Unfortunately, no. — No? Must keep looking then!

items.analysis: A 'deficit economy' stalling tactic. In the USSR, shoppers took whatever was in stock; asking for a specific aesthetic detail like 'pearl buttons' was a polite but ridiculous way to reject an item and linger in a shop without looking like a spy.
